subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Students confuse the rotation angle; the map is rotated 135° clockwise, making south-east become north. Candidates may miscalculate which direction south maps to.
elimination_hint: Identify the rotation: SE→N means a 135° clockwise rotation. Apply the same rotation to South (180°) to get North-East (45°).
factual_hooks: SE becomes N means 135° clockwise rotation
Each compass direction shifts by 135°
contrast_with: Simple 90° rotation questions

subject: History
trap_factor: Statement 2 seems plausible since the manifesto was a business document, but it did NOT get wide all-India business support — it was largely a Bombay/industry-specific document.
elimination_hint: Recall that the Bombay Manifesto (1936) was signed primarily by Bombay-based industrialists opposing socialist tendencies of Congress; Statement 2 overstates its reach.
factual_hooks: Bombay Manifesto 1936 opposed socialist ideals in Congress
Support was largely from Bombay businessmen, not all-India
contrast_with: Karachi Resolution 1931 — which had socialist leanings

subject: Economy
trap_factor: The term 'pumped-storage' alongside 'hydropower' may mislead students toward irrigation or lift irrigation contexts given the water-pumping connotation.
elimination_hint: Pumped-storage hydropower works by pumping water uphill when energy surplus and releasing it downhill when needed — making it a grid-scale long-duration energy storage solution.
factual_hooks: Pumped-storage hydropower is the largest form of grid-scale energy storage globally
It acts as a giant rechargeable battery using water and gravity
contrast_with: Run-of-river hydropower — generates but cannot store energy
contemporary_relevance: Pumped-storage hydropower is gaining attention in India's energy storage strategy under the National Electricity Plan.

subject: History
trap_factor: Visuddhimagga is a famous Buddhist commentary (by Buddhaghosa), making it a strong distractor; Prajnaparamita and Lalitavistara are also well-known Buddhist texts.
elimination_hint: Sanghabhuti was associated with the Sarvastivada school prevalent in NW India at that time; his travel to China to spread Sarvastivada Vinaya is the key link.
factual_hooks: Sanghabhuti — Indian Buddhist monk, 4th century AD
Commentator on Sarvastivada Vinaya
prerequisite_concepts: Buddhist monks who travelled to China
contrast_with: Fa-Hien — Chinese monk who came TO India vs. Sanghabhuti who went to China

subject: History
trap_factor: C. Rajagopalachari is associated with Swatantra Party (not Socialist Party) and Acharya Narendra Dev with Socialist Party — candidates often swap these two pairs.
elimination_hint: Confirm: BJS–Mukherjee (correct) and Congress for Democracy–Jagjivan Ram (correct) = Only two correctly matched, so answer is B.
factual_hooks: BJS founded by Dr. Shyama Prasad Mukherjee in 1951
Swatantra Party led by C. Rajagopalachari, not Narendra Dev
contrast_with: Swatantra Party vs. Socialist Party leadership

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Candidates may eliminate option C easily but not be sure whether UP or Maharashtra publishes the most newspapers; Uttar Pradesh actually leads in newspaper count.
elimination_hint: Statement C is factually incorrect — Uttar Pradesh (not Maharashtra) publishes the largest number of newspapers in India by count.
factual_hooks: Uttar Pradesh leads India in number of newspapers published
Press Council of India is an autonomous quasi-judicial body
contrast_with: Press Council of India (regulatory) vs. Press Trust of India (news agency)

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Candidates may think only one of them is endangered — the mugger crocodile was once critically low but the elephant is also listed as endangered globally (IUCN).
elimination_hint: Both species appear on IUCN endangered or vulnerable lists and are protected under Wildlife Protection Act India; the answer is Both 1 and 2.
factual_hooks: Indian elephant listed as Endangered on IUCN Red List
Gharial (not mugger) is Critically Endangered; saltwater and mugger crocodiles vary
contrast_with: Endangered vs. Critically Endangered species in India

subject: History
trap_factor: Dates of wars are frequently confused: First Anglo-Maratha War was 1775-82 (not 1767-69), and Second Sikh War was 1848-49 (not 1845-46).
elimination_hint: Only pairs 2 (Third Mysore War 1790-92) and 3 (First Anglo-Burmese War 1824-26) are correct — making option D the answer.
factual_hooks: Third Mysore War: 1790-92 (Cornwallis vs. Tipu)
First Anglo-Burmese War: 1824-26
contrast_with: First Anglo-Maratha War (1775-82) vs. Second (1803-05)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Candidates may confuse locations of similar-sounding research institutes; options mix Hyderabad, Mumbai, Coimbatore and Jabalpur plausibly.
elimination_hint: Indian Institute of Geomagnetism is at Mumbai (Navi Mumbai); ARCI (powder metallurgy) is at Hyderabad; SACON is at Coimbatore; TFRI is at Jabalpur.
factual_hooks: SACON (Salim Ali Centre for Ornithology) is in Coimbatore
TFRI (Tropical Forestry Research Institute) is in Jabalpur
contrast_with: ICAR institutes vs. CSIR institutes — different parent bodies

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Candidates may assume metallic minerals dominate due to high visibility of iron ore, but fuel minerals (coal, petroleum) vastly dominate India's mineral value.
elimination_hint: Fuel minerals (coal, petroleum) contribute the largest share of mineral production value in India, followed by metallic, then non-metallic.
factual_hooks: Fuel minerals dominate India's mineral production value
Order: Fuel > Metallic > Non-metallic
contrast_with: Volume vs. value of minerals — coal is high volume AND high value

subject: Economy
trap_factor: The name 'Projects and Development India Limited' sounds generic and could be associated with any sector; options like petroleum refineries seem plausible.
elimination_hint: PDIL (Projects and Development India Limited) is a Government of India enterprise under the fertiliser ministry, specializing in fertiliser and chemical plant engineering.
factual_hooks: PDIL specializes in fertiliser and allied chemical plant design
It is under the Department of Fertilizers, Government of India
contrast_with: EIL (Engineers India Ltd) — petroleum refinery focus

subject: Geography
trap_factor: All three pairs swap countries — Mallorca is Spain, Normandy is France, Sardinia is Italy — candidates may half-know one and assume the others are correct.
elimination_hint: All three are incorrectly matched: Mallorca→Spain, Normandy→France, Sardinia→Italy. Answer is 'None' (D).
factual_hooks: Mallorca is the largest island of Spain in the Mediterranean
Normandy is a region of northern France
Sardinia is an autonomous island region of Italy
contrast_with: Corsica (France) vs. Sardinia (Italy) — both Mediterranean islands

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Stocks and bonds are conventional investments; candidates may confuse hedge funds and venture capital as not being 'Alternative Investment Funds' in the technical regulatory sense.
elimination_hint: Under SEBI's AIF Regulations, hedge funds and venture capital funds are Alternative Investment Funds; bonds and stocks are conventional instruments — only II and IV qualify.
factual_hooks: SEBI regulates Alternative Investment Funds (AIF) in India
Hedge funds and venture capital are classified as AIFs
contrast_with: Conventional investments (stocks, bonds) vs. Alternative Investment Funds
contemporary_relevance: SEBI has been tightening AIF regulations to ensure transparency and investor protection.

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Enforcement Directorate is under Ministry of Finance (not MHA) — this is a common error. DRI and DGSDM details about administrative control trip candidates.
elimination_hint: ED is under the Department of Revenue, Ministry of Finance (not MHA). Only the DRI description (Customs Act + MoF) is correct — so only one statement is correct.
factual_hooks: Enforcement Directorate is under Department of Revenue, Ministry of Finance
DRI enforces Customs Act 1962 under MoF
prerequisite_concepts: Investigative agencies and their parent ministries
contrast_with: ED (financial crimes) vs. CBI (general crimes) — different ministries
contemporary_relevance: ED has been at the centre of high-profile economic offence cases in recent years.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Candidates may think SEBI mandates BRSR (it's actually mandated by SEBI for listed companies, not RBI), making Statement I seem correct at first glance.
elimination_hint: SEBI (not RBI) mandates BRSR for listed companies — Statement I is wrong. Statement II is correct: BRSR disclosures are largely non-financial.
factual_hooks: SEBI mandates BRSR for top 1000 listed companies by market cap
BRSR covers ESG and non-financial disclosures
contrast_with: BRSR (non-financial disclosures) vs. Annual Report (financial disclosures)
contemporary_relevance: BRSR is India's step toward mandatory ESG disclosure aligned with global sustainability standards.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Poultry farming income exemption exists but the basis stated in Statement I (location in rural areas) is oversimplified — all agricultural income is exempt, but poultry is NOT agricultural income for tax purposes.
elimination_hint: Poultry income is NOT exempt as agricultural income under Income Tax Act — Statement I is incorrect. Rural agricultural land not being a capital asset (Statement II) is correct.
factual_hooks: Rural agricultural land is NOT a capital asset under Income Tax Act 1961
Poultry farming income is taxable — not treated as agricultural income
prerequisite_concepts: Capital assets definition under IT Act
contrast_with: Agricultural income (exempt) vs. allied agricultural activities like poultry (taxable)

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Statement II sounds plausible (India lacks critical minerals) but is a trap — India is NOT resource-rich in all 30 identified critical minerals.
elimination_hint: India joined MSP (Statement I correct) and Parliament amended MMDR Act 2023 (Statement III correct); India is NOT self-sufficient in all 30 critical minerals (Statement II wrong). Answer: I and III.
factual_hooks: India is a member of Minerals Security Partnership (MSP)
MMDR Act amended in 2023 for critical mineral auctions
contrast_with: Critical minerals India lacks (lithium, cobalt) vs. abundant (iron ore, coal)
contemporary_relevance: Critical minerals are central to India's EV transition and clean energy goals.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Candidates may overthink whether both Statements II and III are needed or just one; the key is understanding WHY bondholders are at lower risk.
elimination_hint: Bondholders are lenders (II) AND have repayment priority (III) — both together explain why they are at lower risk than stockholders (I). Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Bondholders are creditors; stockholders are equity owners
In liquidation, bondholders are paid before stockholders
contrast_with: Risk-return tradeoff: stockholders bear higher risk but may get higher returns

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Statement III sounds false (SEBI does regulate and warn investors) — candidates may incorrectly rule out Statement III being wrong, but the correct answer includes I and II only.
elimination_hint: SEBI does regulate options trading and act on unregistered advisors — Statement III is factually wrong. Statements I (large share of global equity options) and II (stock market growth) are correct.
factual_hooks: India accounts for a disproportionately large share of global equity options trading
BSE and NSE surpassed Hong Kong in market cap at certain points
contrast_with: Options trading (high risk derivatives) vs. equity cash market
contemporary_relevance: India's derivatives market boom has raised concerns about retail investor losses.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Candidates may think only Statement II or III separately explains Statement I, missing that both together explain GHG reduction (through less raw material use AND less waste).
elimination_hint: Both II (less raw material) and III (less waste) reduce emissions across the production lifecycle, jointly explaining Statement I (GHG reduction). Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Circular economy reduces emissions by minimizing raw material extraction and waste
Circular economy is a key strategy in climate action plans globally
contrast_with: Linear economy (take-make-dispose) vs. circular economy (reuse-recycle-regenerate)
contemporary_relevance: India's National Resource Efficiency Policy promotes circular economy principles.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Statement III is a trap — interest received on loans is a REVENUE receipt (not capital receipt) for the government; it creates no liability.
elimination_hint: Only I (capital receipts create liability) and II (borrowings and disinvestment are capital receipts) are correct. Interest received is a revenue receipt. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Capital receipts: borrowings (liability) + disinvestment (asset reduction)
Interest received on loans = revenue receipt, not capital receipt
contrast_with: Capital receipts (liability-creating) vs. revenue receipts (non-liability)

subject: History
trap_factor: Statement I might seem false as Roy is often portrayed as a modernist who rejected tradition — but he actually deeply respected Indian philosophical traditions (Vedanta, Upanishads).
elimination_hint: Roy was both a lover of Eastern philosophical tradition AND a champion of rationalism/equality — both statements are correct. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Raja Ram Mohan Roy founded Brahmo Samaj and opposed Sati
He had deep knowledge of Vedanta, Persian, Arabic and Western philosophy
contrast_with: Roy vs. Dayananda Saraswati — both reformers but different approaches to tradition

subject: History
trap_factor: Statement III (retention of titles without using them) was NOT part of the programme — the programme called for surrendering titles, not retaining them quietly.
elimination_hint: Items I (boycott of courts/cloth), II (non-violence) and IV (panchayats) were part of NCM; retaining titles (III) was not — so three items are correct. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Non-Cooperation Movement (1920) included surrender of titles, boycott of courts, foreign goods
Non-violence was a cardinal principle of NCM
contrast_with: NCM vs. Civil Disobedience Movement — different methods and targets

subject: History
trap_factor: Options A (leather bag over pulley = rahat) and D (bucket by hand = dhenkli) look plausible; the pot-wheel description matches araghatta but the specific mechanism is tricky.
elimination_hint: Araghatta is a Persian wheel — a large wheel with earthen pots tied to its rim, rotated by bullocks, lifting water continuously. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Araghatta = Persian wheel/pot-wheel, used in medieval and ancient India
Driven by bullocks, pots dip into well and pour water into channel at top
contrast_with: Rahat (simple rope-bucket pulley) vs. Araghatta (continuous pot-wheel)

subject: History
trap_factor: All options are Pallava rulers — narrowing requires knowledge that 'Mattavilasa' (lover of drunken sport) is a play authored by Mahendravarman I himself.
elimination_hint: Mahendravarman I of Pallava dynasty authored 'Mattavilasa Prahasana' and bore the title 'Vichitrachitta'; the titles are a direct clue to his artistic patronage.
factual_hooks: Mahendravarman I (Pallava) = Mattavilasa, Vichitrachitta, Gunabhara
He authored the Sanskrit farce Mattavilasa Prahasana
contrast_with: Narasimhavarman I (Mamalla) — built Mahabalipuram, different titles

subject: History
trap_factor: Fa-hien is often confused with Xuanzang (Hiuen Tsang) — Xuanzang came during Harsha's reign, while Fa-hien came during the Gupta period under Chandragupta II.
elimination_hint: Fa-hien (399-414 AD) visited India during the reign of Chandragupta II (Vikramaditya) — one of the most frequently tested Gupta facts. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Fa-hien visited India 399-414 AD during Chandragupta II's reign
He wrote 'Record of Buddhist Kingdoms' (Foguoji)
contrast_with: Xuanzang came during Harsha's reign (7th century), not Gupta period

subject: History
trap_factor: The maritime campaign against Srivijaya is sometimes wrongly attributed to Rajaraja I; it was his son Rajendra I who conducted the famous overseas naval campaign.
elimination_hint: Rajendra I (Chola) launched the famous naval campaign c.1025 AD against Srivijaya (modern Sumatra/Malay region). Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Rajendra I (Chola) naval campaign against Srivijaya c.1025 AD
Chola navy dominated the Bay of Bengal and Indian Ocean
contrast_with: Rajaraja I (built Brihadesvara Temple) vs. Rajendra I (naval campaigns)

subject: History
trap_factor: Avanti's association with Mahanadi is incorrect (Avanti is in present Madhya Pradesh, associated with Narmada/Shipra), and Kamboja's Vipas (Beas) pairing may be confused with its NW location.
elimination_hint: Asmaka-Godavari (correct) and Kosala-Sarayu (correct) are well-known pairs; Kamboja is NW and Vipas (Beas) is plausible; Avanti-Mahanadi is wrong. Two pairs correct. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Asmaka Mahajanapada was on the banks of Godavari
Kosala Mahajanapada was on the banks of Sarayu (Ghaghra)
contrast_with: Avanti — on Narmada/Kshipra, not Mahanadi

subject: History
trap_factor: Gwalior has a strong music tradition (Gwalior gharana), making it a very attractive distractor; Lahore (then undivided India) is the correct answer.
elimination_hint: Vishnu Digambar Paluskar set up the first Gandharva Mahavidyalaya in Lahore in 1901 — now in Pakistan. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Gandharva Mahavidyalaya founded 1901 in Lahore by Vishnu Digambar Paluskar
Paluskar was a key figure in popularizing Hindustani classical music
contrast_with: Gwalior gharana (music style) ≠ Gandharva Mahavidyalaya (institution)

subject: History
trap_factor: Pradeshika sounds like a village-level officer; Yukta sounds central — but Ashokan inscriptions clearly place all three at the district (janapada/ahara) level.
elimination_hint: Ashokan inscriptions identify Pradeshika, Rajuka, and Yukta as district-level officers responsible for welfare and justice at the district level. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Pradeshika, Rajuka, Yukta = district-level Mauryan officers
Rajukas were responsible for welfare of rural people in districts
contrast_with: Amatya (central level) vs. Pradeshika (district level)

subject: History
trap_factor: Statement II sounds accurate but the resolution mentioned 'swaraj' within a year as a condition before escalating to civil disobedience — Statement I is the only unambiguously correct one.
elimination_hint: The Calcutta session (1920) declared Swaraj as objective by legitimate means (I correct). Civil disobedience was the NEXT stage if Swaraj wasn't achieved — but the Non-cooperation Programme itself did NOT include non-payment of taxes as the immediate next step (II oversimplifies). Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Non-Cooperation Movement 1920: objective was Swaraj by legitimate and peaceful means
CDM (non-payment of taxes) was a later escalation, not part of original NCM
contrast_with: NCM (1920-22) vs. CDM (1930) — different stages of Gandhi's struggle

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Austria and Serbia are often assumed to be NATO members due to European politics; Austria is officially neutral and Serbia is not a member.
elimination_hint: NATO members from the list: Bulgaria, Croatia, Sweden (joined 2024), North Macedonia = 4 members. Austria (neutral) and Serbia (not member) are excluded. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Sweden joined NATO in March 2024
North Macedonia joined NATO in 2020
Austria is permanently neutral — not a NATO member
contrast_with: NATO members vs. EU members — overlap but not identical
contemporary_relevance: Sweden's NATO membership was a significant geopolitical shift following Russia's invasion of Ukraine.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Candidates may include Brazil (Andes runs along its western neighbors, not through Brazil) or exclude Colombia (Andes runs through it extensively).
elimination_hint: Andes passes through: Bolivia, Colombia, Ecuador, and Venezuela (western edge). Brazil and Paraguay are excluded — the Andes doesn't pass through them. Four countries = Answer C.
factual_hooks: Andes mountains run along the western spine of South America
Andes passes through Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, Bolivia, Chile, Argentina
contrast_with: Andes (western SA) vs. Brazilian Highlands (eastern/central SA)

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Candidates may think the Supreme Court can give advisory opinions on its own initiative (like a constitutional court); under Article 143, it can only do so when the President SEEKS advice.
elimination_hint: Article 143: President can seek Supreme Court's advisory opinion; SC cannot give it suo motu or restrict it to Fundamental Rights alone. Answer: B (only statement 2 is correct).
factual_hooks: Article 143: President may refer matters to Supreme Court for advisory opinion
SC advisory opinion is not binding on the President
contrast_with: Advisory jurisdiction (Article 143) vs. Original jurisdiction (Article 131)

subject: Polity
trap_factor: All three statements about Lok Adalats contain plausible-sounding but incorrect restrictions — candidates may pick B or C thinking one statement is correct.
elimination_hint: Lok Adalats can handle pre-litigation AND pending matters; can deal with criminal compoundable offences; can include lay persons alongside judicial officers — all three statements are wrong. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Lok Adalats have jurisdiction over both pre-litigation and court-pending matters
They can handle criminal compoundable offences (not just civil)
contrast_with: Lok Adalat (no appeal possible) vs. regular courts (appellate structure)
contemporary_relevance: Lok Adalats are central to India's ADR framework to reduce pendency in courts.

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Candidates may only count ordered triplets of factors of 30 without accounting for all permutations including repeated values, undercounting arrangements.
elimination_hint: Find all ordered triplets (a,b,c) where a×b×c=30. Factor sets: (1,1,30),(1,2,15),(1,3,10),(1,5,6),(2,3,5) — count permutations of each, total = 3+6+6+6+6 = 27. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: 30 = 2×3×5; factor triplet enumeration needed
Permutation count for each distinct triplet must be calculated separately
contrast_with: Combination (order doesn't matter) vs. Permutation (order matters for distribution)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Statement 2 contains a trap — tea can be propagated by both seeds and stem cuttings, not stem cuttings only; candidates may accept both statements as correct.
elimination_hint: Statement 1 is correct (coffee = tropical; tea = tropical + subtropical). Statement 2 is wrong — tea is also propagated by seeds in some methods. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Coffee requires hot, humid tropical climate; tea grows in tropical and subtropical regions
Tea is propagated primarily by vegetative cuttings but seeds are also used
contrast_with: Coffee (tropical only) vs. tea (tropical + subtropical hill slopes)

subject: Governance
trap_factor: AIDS may seem the obvious answer given historical awareness campaigns; candidates may overlook tuberculosis as the historically larger global killer by WHO data.
elimination_hint: TB has long been the leading infectious disease killer globally per WHO data; the question is based on 1996 WHO data where TB surpassed AIDS as the single largest killer.
factual_hooks: Tuberculosis remains one of the top infectious disease killers globally
WHO data (1990s) showed TB deaths exceeding AIDS deaths annually
contrast_with: AIDS (HIV/AIDS epidemic) vs. TB (bacterial, vaccine-preventable)

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Option D (disinflation — falling rate of inflation) is a common confusion with deflation; option B (recession) conflates economic output contraction with price levels.
elimination_hint: Deflation = persistent fall in general price level (not just rate of inflation). Option C is the precise definition; option D describes disinflation. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Deflation = sustained decline in the general price level
Disinflation ≠ Deflation (disinflation = falling inflation rate, still positive)
contrast_with: Deflation (negative inflation) vs. Disinflation (slowing inflation still positive)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Option B (man doesn't develop immunity) sounds plausible but is incorrect — humans do develop partial immunity. Option D (intermediate host) is factually true but not the main reason.
elimination_hint: The primary challenge for malaria vaccine development is multiple Plasmodium species with different antigens; no single vaccine covers all. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Malaria caused by 5 species of Plasmodium (falciparum, vivax, etc.)
Antigenic variation across Plasmodium species makes universal vaccine difficult
contrast_with: Bacterial vaccines (single pathogen) vs. malarial vaccine (multiple parasite species)
contemporary_relevance: RTS,S/AS01 (Mosquirix) is the first approved malaria vaccine, targeting P. falciparum only.

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: The two-row, three-each arrangement with diagonal opposite condition is tricky; candidates may misplace P and O, leading to wrong identification of who faces N.
elimination_hint: Map positions: Q is middle seat, O is Q's neighbour (diagonally opposite P). N is R's neighbour. Working through constraints places Q facing N. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Seating arrangement: fix Q at centre first since Q is not at any row end
Diagonal opposite means across rows and across one seat
contrast_with: Circular vs. linear seating — different adjacency rules

subject: Economy
trap_factor: GDR (Global Depositary Receipt) is confused with artificial currency; ADR and GDR are real instruments representing real shares, not artificial currencies.
elimination_hint: SDR (Special Drawing Rights) is an international reserve asset created by IMF — an artificial/supplementary currency. ADR and GDR are depositary receipts, not currencies. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: SDR (Special Drawing Rights) = artificial reserve currency created by IMF in 1969
ADR/GDR = depositary receipts for shares, NOT currencies
contrast_with: SDR (international reserve asset) vs. cryptocurrency (decentralised digital currency)

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: ISO (International Organization for Standardization) is a very plausible distractor as it sets global standards; Codex Alimentarius is less well-known but is the actual WTO reference body.
elimination_hint: WTO's SPS Agreement (Sanitary and Phytosanitary) recognizes Codex Alimentarius Commission as the standard-setting body for food safety. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Codex Alimentarius Commission = joint FAO/WHO body setting food safety standards
WTO SPS Agreement references Codex for food safety disputes
contrast_with: Codex Alimentarius (food safety) vs. ISO (general technical standards)

subject: History
trap_factor: Annulment of partition of Bengal seems like it should be a resolution given the Swadeshi context, but it was NOT passed as a formal resolution at the 1906 Calcutta session.
elimination_hint: The four Calcutta 1906 resolutions were: Swaraj, Swadeshi, Boycott, and National Education. 'Annulment of partition' was NOT one of them — it was the underlying cause but not a formal resolution. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Calcutta 1906 INC: four resolutions — Swaraj, Swadeshi, Boycott, National Education
Surat split 1907 occurred over acceptance/rejection of these resolutions
contrast_with: Calcutta 1906 (moderate-extremist tension) vs. Surat 1907 (formal split)

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Japan is a common wrong guess given its proximity to Korea; Vietnam and Cambodia are plausible distractors for Asian peace laureates.
elimination_hint: Kim Dae-jung was the President of South Korea who received the 2000 Nobel Peace Prize for his Sunshine Policy toward North Korea. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Kim Dae-jung — Nobel Peace Prize 2000, President of South Korea
Known for 'Sunshine Policy' of engagement with North Korea
contrast_with: Kim Dae-jung (South Korea) vs. Kim Jong-il (North Korea)

subject: Governance
trap_factor: The context is circa 2008; Roopa Farooki as a fiction writer (British-Bangladeshi) is obscure; candidates may confuse K.P. Singh (DLF real estate) with banking.
elimination_hint: K.P. Singh = DLF (real estate), Vikram Pandit = Citigroup CEO (banking), Roopa Farooki = fiction writer, Kishore Biyani = Future Group (retail). Answer: B (A-4, B-1, C-2, D-3).
factual_hooks: K.P. Singh = Chairman of DLF (real estate)
Vikram Pandit = CEO of Citigroup (banking)

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Sunil Gavaskar and Mohammad Azharuddin are equally strong distractors; Pataudi led India for many years but Ganguly led for more test matches as captain.
elimination_hint: Sourav Ganguly captained India in 49 Test matches (most at time of question in 2008). Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Sourav Ganguly captained India in 49 Test matches
Ganguly known for transforming Indian cricket's aggressive away performances

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: James Watson is a Nobel laureate often associated vaguely with 'science' — candidates may guess meteorology or environmental science without knowing the DNA discovery.
elimination_hint: James Watson co-discovered the double helix structure of DNA with Francis Crick (Nobel 1962) — genetics is the unambiguous answer. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: James D. Watson won Nobel Prize 1962 (Physiology/Medicine) for DNA double helix
Co-discoverers: Watson, Crick, and Wilkins

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: All three statements contain extreme/absolute words like 'No virus can...', which are almost always false in science — yet candidates may accept them as plausible.
elimination_hint: Viruses DO survive in ocean (marine viromes), DO infect bacteria (bacteriophages), and DO affect host cell transcription. All three are false — answer is None. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Bacteriophages are viruses that infect bacteria
Oceans contain vast viral diversity (marine virome)
Viruses hijack host cell transcriptional machinery
contrast_with: Viruses (acellular) vs. bacteria (cellular organisms)
contemporary_relevance: Marine viromes play a crucial ecological role in ocean carbon cycling.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Candidates may doubt Statement II (Ghana's gold from Brazil plateau) as it sounds very specific, but all three are legitimate evidences of continental drift.
elimination_hint: All three (matching rock belts, gold deposits, and Gondwana sediments) are textbook evidences for continental drift theory. Answer: C (I, II and III).
factual_hooks: Continental drift evidence: geological rock continuity across oceans
Gondwana system sediments found across 6 southern hemisphere landmasses
contrast_with: Continental drift (Wegener) vs. Plate tectonics (modern theory)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Statement II claims subtropical areas have less dry winds — this is the opposite of reality (subtropical regions are associated with dry, high-pressure belts). Statement I is also debatable.
elimination_hint: Statement I (more dust in subtropical/temperate than equatorial/polar) is correct. Statement II is incorrect — subtropical regions have more dry winds, not less. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Subtropical high-pressure zones produce dry, dust-carrying winds
Equatorial regions have high humidity reducing dust suspension
contrast_with: Equatorial low (wet, humid) vs. subtropical high (dry, dusty)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: The direction of isotherm bending (equatorward over land in January) is counterintuitive — in winter (January NH), land is colder so isotherms bend toward the equator over continents.
elimination_hint: In January (NH winter), land cools faster than oceans; oceans are warmer (Statement II correct), causing isotherms to bend equatorward over land and poleward over oceans (Statement I correct); II explains I. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: In January NH: isotherms bend equatorward over continents (colder), poleward over oceans (warmer)
Land heats and cools faster than water — differential heating principle
prerequisite_concepts: Differential heating of land and water
contrast_with: January pattern (land colder) vs. July pattern (land hotter) in NH

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Statement III ('clay is not at all porous') is incorrect — clay is actually porous but impermeable because its pores are tiny and poorly connected; candidates may accept this false statement.
elimination_hint: Statement II (chalk is porous → permeable) is correct. Statement III (clay is not porous) is wrong — clay is porous but impermeable. Only II explains I, making Answer B correct.
factual_hooks: Chalk is permeable because it is porous — water moves through it
Clay is porous but impermeable — tiny pores don't allow water flow
contrast_with: Porosity (capacity to hold water) vs. permeability (capacity to transmit water)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Statement III uses 'radiation' specifically — candidates may think CO2 absorbs heat but not 'radiation' specifically; in fact, greenhouse gases absorb infrared radiation (long-wave).
elimination_hint: All three statements about atmosphere and greenhouse effect are correct. Without atmosphere → freezing; atmosphere traps heat → maintains temperature; CO2 absorbs radiation. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Earth's average temperature maintained by greenhouse effect of atmosphere
CO2 and other GHGs absorb long-wave (infrared) radiation from Earth's surface
contrast_with: Natural greenhouse effect (essential) vs. enhanced greenhouse effect (climate change)
contemporary_relevance: Understanding the greenhouse effect is foundational to climate change policy discussions.

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Candidates may think Statement I is wrong because the scheme is about cattle breed improvement, not poverty — but the scheme explicitly targets small/marginal farmers who own low-productivity indigenous breeds.
elimination_hint: Both statements are correct: Rashtriya Gokul Mission targets indigenous cattle/buffalo conservation (II) and benefits small/marginal farmers and landless laborers (I). Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Rashtriya Gokul Mission launched 2014 for indigenous cattle breed development
Focuses on conservation and development of indigenous breeds like Gir, Sahiwal
contrast_with: Rashtriya Gokul Mission (indigenous breeds) vs. National Livestock Mission (all livestock)
contemporary_relevance: Aligns with India's focus on natural farming and indigenous breed conservation.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Statement III (limestone → lime in clinker production) is correct but Statement II has a nuance — silica is typically added via materials like clay OR shale, and the process detail may trip candidates.
elimination_hint: Statement II (silica-bearing clay + limestone) and III (limestone → lime in clinker) are both correct, but only Statement III directly explains CO2 emissions from cement. Answer: B (both correct, only one explains).
factual_hooks: Cement industry contributes ~8% of global CO2 emissions
Clinker production: CaCO3 (limestone) → CaO (lime) + CO2 (major emission source)
contrast_with: Cement emissions (process-based) vs. energy-based industrial emissions
contemporary_relevance: Cement decarbonization is a major challenge in India's net-zero transition.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Statement II (COP28 declaration is binding) is a trap — most COP declarations are non-binding; candidates familiar with this may still be uncertain about India's specific reason for not signing.
elimination_hint: The COP28 Climate and Health Declaration is NOT binding (Statement II wrong); India didn't sign due to sovereignty/cost concerns (Statement III is debatable). As both II and III are incorrect, Answer: D (neither).
factual_hooks: COP28 Declaration on Climate and Health is voluntary/non-binding
India did not sign the COP28 health declaration
contrast_with: Paris Agreement (binding targets) vs. COP28 health declaration (voluntary)
contemporary_relevance: COP28 health and climate linkage discussions are gaining importance globally.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Solar flares bombarding the outer atmosphere sounds like it would affect Earth's rotation; in fact it is melting polar ice (water redistributing to equator) that affects Earth's axis — Statement II is irrelevant.
elimination_hint: Earth's axis shift is explained by polar ice melt causing mass redistribution toward equator (Statement III) — NOT solar flares (Statement II). Only Statement III explains I. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Melting polar ice redistributes water toward equator, shifting Earth's axis of rotation
Solar flares affect ionosphere/magnetosphere, not Earth's rotation axis
contrast_with: Axial tilt (obliquity) vs. wobble in rotation axis due to mass redistribution
contemporary_relevance: Scientific studies report measurable shifts in Earth's rotation axis due to climate change.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Statement III says Article 6 promotes inter-country 'non-market' strategies — but Article 6 is primarily about market mechanisms (carbon markets), with Article 6.8 covering non-market approaches.
elimination_hint: Article 6 sets principles for carbon markets (II correct) AND includes non-market approaches via Article 6.8 (III also correct) — both explain why Article 6 is widely discussed. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Article 6 Paris Agreement: establishes international carbon market rules
Article 6.8 covers non-market cooperative approaches
contrast_with: Article 6 (carbon markets) vs. NDCs (nationally determined contributions) — Article 4
contemporary_relevance: COP29 (Baku, 2024) finalized Article 6 carbon market rules.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: AIIB and NDB are newer multilateral banks more associated with sustainable infrastructure in Asia; ADB is the correct answer but candidates may confuse it with these newer institutions.
elimination_hint: ADB (Asian Development Bank) launched the Nature Solutions Finance Hub for Asia-Pacific as part of its biodiversity and nature finance initiatives. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: ADB launched Nature Solutions Finance Hub for Asia and Pacific
ADB focuses on sustainable development across developing Asia
contrast_with: ADB (regional bank for Asia) vs. AIIB (infrastructure focus) vs. NDB (BRICS bank)
contemporary_relevance: Nature-based solutions financing is gaining prominence in post-Kunming-Montreal biodiversity framework.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Statement III about synthetic aviation fuel (SAF) may seem incorrect to candidates unfamiliar with power-to-X technologies; all three uses of DAC are valid.
elimination_hint: Direct Air Capture can be used for: carbon sequestration (I), feedstock for plastics and food processing CO2 (II), and synthetic fuel for aviation via CO2+H2 (III). All correct. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Direct Air Capture (DAC) removes CO2 directly from ambient air
DAC captured CO2 can make synthetic aviation fuels, plastics, and food-grade CO2
contrast_with: DAC (point-of-air capture) vs. CCS (point-of-source capture at industrial plants)
contemporary_relevance: DAC is considered essential for net-zero targets but remains expensive at scale.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Statement I calls it an 'omnivorous crustacean' — Peacock tarantula is an arachnid (spider), not a crustacean, making Statement I doubly wrong.
elimination_hint: Peacock tarantula is NOT a crustacean (it's a spider/arachnid); it IS limited to Andhra Pradesh forests (II correct) and IS arboreal (tree-dwelling) (III correct). Answer: D (II and III).
factual_hooks: Gooty tarantula (Poecilotheria metallica) is found only in Andhra Pradesh forests
It is an arboreal (tree-dwelling) spider, not a ground dweller
contrast_with: Arboreal species (tree-dwelling) vs. terrestrial species (ground-dwelling)
contemporary_relevance: Gooty tarantula is critically endangered due to habitat loss and illegal pet trade.

subject: History
trap_factor: The National Conference founding in 1883 at Calcutta is confused with Congress founding (1885); Muslim League (1906) and Communal Award (1932) may be mismatched with other dates.
elimination_hint: 1883=National Conference (D); 1906=Muslim League at Dacca (C); 1927=All India States Peoples Conference (B); 1932=Communal Award (A). Match: I-D, II-C, III-B, IV-A. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Muslim League founded 1906 at Dacca
Communal Award announced 1932 by Ramsay MacDonald
contrast_with: Communal Award (1932) vs. Poona Pact (1932) — both related to separate electorates

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Statement I claims India's per capita CO2 is less than 0.5t — India's actual per capita emissions are around 1.9t CO2 (2024), making Statement I clearly wrong.
elimination_hint: India's per capita CO2 is ~1.9t (well above 0.5t) — Statement I is wrong. India ranks among top 3 globally in total CO2 but NOT necessarily second in Asia-Pacific (China leads). Statements II and III are correct. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: India's per capita CO2 emissions ~1.9 tonnes per year
Electricity and heat production is the largest CO2 source in India
contrast_with: Per capita emissions (India low) vs. total emissions (India 3rd globally)
contemporary_relevance: India's emissions data is critical for climate negotiations and NDC commitments.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Papaya described as a 'woody shrub' and Mint as an 'annual shrub' are the key traps — papaya is a herbaceous tree, and mint is a perennial herb, not a shrub.
elimination_hint: Cassava (woody shrub ✓), Ginger (herb with pseudostem ✓), Malabar spinach (herbaceous climber ✓) are correct. Mint (perennial, not annual shrub) and Papaya (tree, not woody shrub) are wrong. Three correct. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Cassava = woody perennial shrub in tropical areas
Ginger = herbaceous plant with underground rhizome, pseudostem above ground
contrast_with: Papaya (giant herb/tree) vs. woody shrubs (cassava)

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Statement I (rainforests produce more O2 than oceans) seems intuitively correct given their fame, but marine phytoplankton produce ~50% of Earth's oxygen — making Statement I incorrect.
elimination_hint: Oceans (via phytoplankton) produce ~50% of Earth's oxygen (Statement II correct). Rainforests are net near-zero O2 producers (consumed by decomposers). Statement I is wrong. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Marine phytoplankton produce ~50% of Earth's oxygen
Rainforests are not the primary oxygen source — their respiration roughly balances their photosynthesis
contrast_with: Rainforest (net carbon sink) vs. ocean phytoplankton (primary O2 producer)
contemporary_relevance: Ocean health directly affects global oxygen supply — ocean acidification threatens phytoplankton.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Candidates may think fuel cell-electric hybrid is not a standard category; but all three — BEV, FCEV, and FCHEV — are recognized alternative powertrain vehicle types.
elimination_hint: All three types (full battery EV, hydrogen fuel cell, fuel cell-electric hybrid) are classified as alternative powertrain vehicles. Answer: C (all three).
factual_hooks: Alternative powertrain vehicles = any vehicle not using conventional ICE with fossil fuel
Hydrogen fuel cell vehicles emit only water vapour
contrast_with: ICE (conventional) vs. BEV/FCEV (alternative powertrains)
contemporary_relevance: India's FAME scheme and green hydrogen mission support alternative powertrain adoption.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Candidates may think some UAVs (like fixed-wing) can hover or do vertical landing — fixed-wing UAVs cannot hover or do vertical landing; battery is not the only power source (fuel-powered drones exist).
elimination_hint: Not all UAVs do vertical landing (fixed-wing cannot), not all can hover (fixed-wing cannot), and not all use batteries only (many use fuel). All three statements are wrong. Answer: D (None).
factual_hooks: Fixed-wing UAVs cannot hover or do vertical landing
UAVs can be powered by batteries, fuel, or hybrid systems
contrast_with: Multirotor UAV (hovers, vertical landing) vs. Fixed-wing UAV (cannot hover)
contemporary_relevance: UAVs are increasingly used in surveillance, agriculture, and defence applications in India.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Graphite is the anode material in most lithium-ion batteries — candidates may include it as a cathode material; only Cobalt, Lithium, and Nickel typically form cathode composition (NMC/NCA cathodes).
elimination_hint: Battery cathodes typically contain Cobalt (I), Lithium (III), and Nickel (IV). Graphite (II) is used in the ANODE, not cathode. Three elements (I, III, IV) form cathodes. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: NMC cathode = Nickel, Manganese, Cobalt + Lithium
Graphite is the anode material in lithium-ion batteries
contrast_with: Cathode materials (NMC, NCA) vs. anode materials (graphite, silicon)
contemporary_relevance: India's critical mineral policy focuses on securing cobalt, lithium and nickel for EV battery supply chain.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Coal gasification produces syngas (CO + H2); ethanol and urea can be made from syngas but nitroglycerine cannot — candidates may assume all three can be derived.
elimination_hint: Syngas from coal gasification can produce: ethanol (via fermentation/catalytic conversion) and urea (via ammonia synthesis). Nitroglycerine requires glycerol + nitric acid — cannot be produced from coal gasification. Two substances. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Coal gasification produces syngas (CO + H2)
Urea made from ammonia (from syngas); nitroglycerine is not a syngas product
contrast_with: Syngas products (ammonia, methanol, ethanol) vs. nitroglycerine (different synthesis route)
contemporary_relevance: Coal gasification with CCS is being explored as a transition technology in India.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: The obscure acronyms CL-20, HMX, LLM-105 may lead candidates toward rocket propulsion or HFC alternatives; all three are actually high-energy military explosives.
elimination_hint: CL-20 (HNIW), HMX, and LLM-105 are all high-performance military explosive compounds. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: CL-20 (HNIW) is one of the most powerful non-nuclear explosives
HMX (Octogen) is used in nuclear weapon detonators and military explosives
contrast_with: Military explosives (CL-20, HMX) vs. propellants (used in missile propulsion)
contemporary_relevance: India's DRDO works on high-energy materials for defence applications.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Majorana 1 chip was introduced by Microsoft (not Amazon Web Services) — Statement II is the key trap. Statement III (deep learning is subset of ML) is unambiguously true.
elimination_hint: Majorana 1 chip → quantum computing (I correct); it is from Microsoft NOT AWS (II wrong); deep learning is a subset of ML (III correct). Answer: C (I and III).
factual_hooks: Microsoft introduced Majorana 1, a topological quantum chip
Deep learning is a subset of machine learning (which is a subset of AI)
contrast_with: Quantum computing (Majorana/qubits) vs. classical computing (transistors/bits)
contemporary_relevance: Quantum computing and AI are among the most transformative emerging technologies of this decade.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Candidates may doubt that activated carbon is synthesized from environmental waste — but it can be made from agricultural waste (rice husk, coconut shells) with high carbon content.
elimination_hint: All three statements about activated carbon are correct: large surface area (II), adsorbs heavy metals (II), and can be made from carbon-rich environmental waste (III). Both II and III explain its pollution-removal properties (I). Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Activated carbon has extremely high surface area (~500-1500 m²/g)
Can be produced from agricultural waste (coconut shell, rice husk)
contrast_with: Activated carbon (adsorption) vs. ion exchange resins (chemical exchange)
contemporary_relevance: Activated carbon from agricultural waste is a sustainable approach to effluent treatment.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Statement II (Ordinance can abridge Fundamental Rights) is the trap — an Ordinance cannot override or abridge Fundamental Rights, as it must be consistent with the Constitution.
elimination_hint: Ordinances CAN amend Central Acts (I correct) and CAN have retrospective effect/back date (III correct). Ordinances CANNOT abridge Fundamental Rights (II wrong). Answer: C (I and III).
factual_hooks: Ordinances can amend any Central Act (including constitutional ones with limitations)
Ordinances can be retrospective but cannot violate Fundamental Rights
contrast_with: Ordinance (executive law-making) vs. Act of Parliament (legislative law-making)
contemporary_relevance: Ordinance-making power and its limits remain a contested constitutional question.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Inter-State Council sounds constitutional and it IS (Article 263), but Zonal Councils (statutory) and National Security Council (executive order) are NOT constitutional bodies — candidates may think all three are constitutional.
elimination_hint: Only the Inter-State Council is established under the Constitution (Article 263). Zonal Councils = States Reorganisation Act 1956 (statutory). NSC = executive order. Only one (ISC) is constitutional. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Inter-State Council established under Article 263 of the Constitution
Zonal Councils are statutory bodies under States Reorganisation Act 1956
contrast_with: Constitutional bodies (Article-based) vs. Statutory bodies (Act-based) vs. Executive bodies (order-based)
contemporary_relevance: Debates on cooperative federalism often revolve around the functioning of Inter-State Council.
